network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature-guided conclusion that prebiotic arginine synthesis is unresolved and therefore represents a connected scenario graph rather than a fully validated continuous synthesis. The best-supported constructive branch is the surface cyanosulfidic route of Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017), which proceeds from HCN, acetylene, NH3, H2S, Cu photoredox chemistry, phosphate buffer, and UV light at 35C under anaerobic conditions to an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor, followed by hydrolysis to arginine. A second major branch is direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide, historically demonstrated by Schulze & Winterstein (1899) and chemically validated in water by Tsubokura et al. (2014), but still lacking a demonstrated prebiotic end-to-end source of ornithine. Additional low-confidence branches are explicitly marked speculative: a prebiotic urea-cycle analog via citrulline/cyanate, trace supercritical CO2/H2O formation from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine, and downstream stabilization/usage branches. Hydrothermal chemistry is restricted to literature-consistent feedstock provisioning (formate/acetate/pyruvate and alanine formation) and not overstated as a productive high-temperature arginine environment, consistent with arginine lability in hotter hydrothermal systems.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; anoxic; H2-rich

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to formate on Fe-S phases under hydrothermal redox gradients.
reactions.reasoning: Included as conservative hydrothermal feedstock provisioning consistent with Fe-S mediated CO2 reduction in alkaline vent scenarios and the structural relevance of greigite to ferredoxin-like catalysis; used here only as background carbon fixation, not as a direct arginine-forming step (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Jordan et al., 2021; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal carbon fixation from formate to acetate

conditions.other: anoxic subseafloor or vent wall setting

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction and C-C bond-forming surface chemistry generates acetate-like products from reduced one-carbon feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: Used as a plausible hydrothermal provisioning step compatible with Fischer-Tropsch-type and vent carbon-fixation chemistry; this branch is deliberately limited to precursor supply rather than arginine synthesis, in line with the literature caution about arginine instability in hydrothermal settings (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Amend & Shock, 1998; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate from reduced carbon feedstocks

conditions.other: anoxic; proposed vent carbon fixation analog

reactions.mechanism: Fe-mineral mediated reductive carbon fixation and C-C bond formation yields alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: This is a network-connecting hydrothermal alpha-keto acid node motivated by prebiotic carbon-fixation analogs, but it is less directly established than the surface cyanosulfidic arginine branch and is therefore treated as supporting background chemistry only (Kitadai, 2015; Amend & Shock, 1998).

reactions.name: Mineral-mediated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence iron mineral surfaces yielding magnetite
conditions.other: anoxic; Fe2+/Fe3+ mineral-mediated reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: Surface-assisted reductive amination of the alpha-keto acid produces alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Directly grounded in iron-mineral reductive amination literature and retained as hydrothermal/prebiotic metabolic context, not as a direct route to arginine (Barge et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic cross-coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile
agents: sulfide minerals (molybdenite/stibnite analog for cyanosulfidic chemistry)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system with mineral support
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, phosphate-buffered, UV irradiation; H2S present as reductant

reactions.mechanism: Cu photoredox cyanosulfidic chemistry couples HCN-derived carbon with acetylene to produce acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first key literature-supported constructive step toward the arginine precursor under cyanosulfidic protometabolism conditions; mineral agents are included as plausible surface supports while preserving the reported Cu/H2S/UV/phosphate chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Ammonia addition to acrylonitrile forming beta-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces under cyanosulfidic conditions
conditions.other: anaerobic aqueous pool; compatible with Patel et al. network

reactions.mechanism: Conjugate addition of ammonia to the activated alkene nitrile gives beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step follows the reported cyanosulfidic arginine-precursor route in which acrylonitrile is converted to beta-aminopropionitrile; clay/mineral surfaces are included for concentration in surface settings as required by the network specification (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven photoredox with sulfide/Cu-compatible cyanosulfidic setting
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, H2S-assisted reduction in shallow pool

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the nitrile functionality followed by hydrolytic adjustment gives the corresponding amino aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The literature specifically places beta-aminopropionaldehyde downstream of beta-aminopropionitrile in the cyanosulfidic route, although the precise isolated step is less quantified than earlier transformations; therefore this node is included but interpreted cautiously as part of the Patel/Tsanakopoulou sequence rather than a separately optimized standalone transformation (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: UV photochemical formation of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric gases

conditions.other: represents reduced-gas photochemistry in shallow anoxic surface environments; exact gas mixture includes CH4/NH3/H2O per literature

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of reduced nitrogen/carbon gas mixtures generates cyanamide, which accumulates in surface settings.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide is a required guanylating agent in the arginine problem and is literature-supported as forming photochemically from reduced gas mixtures, though not from a single simple stoichiometric aqueous step; this node is included as a surface-source abstraction explicitly grounded in the reported atmospheric photochemistry and cyanosulfidic network role (Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide to guanidine

conditions.other: pH 8.4-8.5; 254 nm UV; days to weeks

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical transformation of ammonium cyanide solutions generates guanidine as a stable nitrogen-rich product.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction follows the classic finding that UV-irradiated ammonium cyanide yields guanidine in significant yield; it provides an independently supported guanidino-pool node but not a validated arginine-forming route by itself (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Efficient guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide and progression to arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox compatible cyanosulfidic network on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, phosphate-buffered, UV irradiation; H2S present

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine addition to cyanamide installs the guanidino functionality, and subsequent cyanosulfidic photoreduction/HCN incorporation yields the reported arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: This is the core target-forming branch in the literature: Patel et al. state that the especially efficient reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide suggests production of compound 46, the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland confirmed the route context. Because quantitative yields were not reported, the step is retained with explicit caution but remains the best-supported constructive path (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pool

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile to the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is standard prebiotic logic and is explicitly the final step implied for compound 46 in the cyanosulfidic arginine branch; this is the best literature-grounded completion to arginine itself, despite lack of a single continuous end-to-end experiment from primitive feedstocks (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.catalyst: base or aqueous activation; no strictly prebiotic catalyst established
conditions.other: historic Ba(OH)2-promoted chemistry; modern aqueous guanylation can reach 100C with non-prebiotic scandium triflate

reactions.mechanism: The terminal primary amine of ornithine attacks cyanamide, directly installing the guanidino group to yield arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic two-phase arginine proposal and the most straightforward conceptual route once ornithine and cyanamide are present. It is historically demonstrated by Schulze & Winterstein and chemically validated in water by Tsubokura et al., but remains incomplete as a fully prebiotic continuous route because ornithine sourcing and prebiotic catalysis are unresolved (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014; Johnson et al., 2008; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis supplying cyanate, urea, and citrulline-bearing products

conditions.other: represents complex HCN oligomerization/hydrolysis mixtures rather than a single clean stoichiometric step

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis generates a complex nitrogen-rich product pool containing citrulline, cyanate, and urea-related species.
reactions.reasoning: This node is intentionally framed as a mixture-generating abstraction, not a precise single-step transformation, because the literature reports citrulline in HCN oligomer hydrolysates and recognizes cyanate/urea as related hydrolysis products without defining a simple stoichiometric route. It is included only to connect the speculative urea-cycle analog branch faithfully and cautiously (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Speculative carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate to citrulline

conditions.other: prebiotic pool; cyanate used as carbamoyl surrogate

reactions.mechanism: The terminal amine of ornithine reacts with cyanate to form a ureido side chain, producing citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is a literature-motivated hypothesis based on cyanate's greater kinetic stability relative to carbamoyl phosphate and the proposal of a prebiotic urea-cycle analog. It has conceptual support but no full experimental validation in a prebiotic arginine network, so it is explicitly marked speculative (Juarez et al., 2021; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Speculative conversion of citrulline to arginine in the presence of cyanamide

conditions.other: hypothetical pool chemistry; no direct experimental demonstration

reactions.mechanism: Proposed guanyl/activation chemistry would convert the ureido-bearing side chain of citrulline to the guanidino group of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is retained only as a clearly speculative branch reflecting literature discussion of a prebiotic urea-cycle analog. No established prebiotic experiment has demonstrated citrulline plus cyanamide to arginine, so this reaction should be interpreted as an exploratory placeholder for an open mechanistic problem (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Trace supercritical CO2/H2O formation of arginine from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine

conditions.other: 60C supercritical CO2/H2O; product detected after acid hydrolysis; trace yield

reactions.mechanism: Unresolved multistep chemistry under supercritical CO2/H2O gives trace arginine after hydrolytic workup.
reactions.reasoning: This low-confidence side branch is included because Fujioka et al. reported trace arginine from hydroxylamine hydrochloride and pyruvic acid under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions. It is not treated as a robust route because the mechanism is unresolved, the starting materials are advanced intermediates, and the yield is tiny relative to alanine (Fujioka et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Biochemical transamination/guanidination analog from pyruvate-derived carbonyl chemistry to arginine-like guanidino acid

conditions.other: speculative concentrated pool chemistry

reactions.mechanism: A speculative combination of alpha-keto acid amination and guanidino-group incorporation yields a guanidino dicarboxylic intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: This connector reaction is explicitly speculative and is included only to provide a low-confidence convergence between hydrothermal pyruvate chemistry and guanidine availability. There is no direct literature demonstration for this specific step in arginine synthesis; it addresses the open systems-level question of how guanidino chemistry might couple to central carbon intermediates without asserting validation (Lohrmann, 1972; Barge et al., 2019; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Decarboxylative conversion of guanidino dicarboxylic intermediate to arginine

conditions.other: thermal decarboxylation in drying environment; highly speculative

reactions.mechanism: Thermal loss of one carboxyl group from a guanidino dicarboxylic precursor yields arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is another explicitly speculative connector included to create a systems-level route from pyruvate-centered chemistry to arginine while acknowledging the literature gap. It is not experimentally established and should be treated as a hypothesis branch only (Fujioka et al., 2009; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite-mediated peptide bond formation from arginine to arginylarginine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles; concentration on clay interlayers

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated arginine on clay interlayers undergoes dehydration-condensation to peptide-linked products.
reactions.reasoning: This downstream utilization step is included because montmorillonite strongly adsorbs arginine and related cationic amino acids and clay-associated arginine complexes can undergo peptide bond formation under dry heating. It is not part of target synthesis itself but provides the required biochemical continuation of the network (Catalano et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2019; Campbell et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite-assisted concentration and protection of arginine

conditions.other: pH 5-7; cation exchange protects guanidinium-bearing amino acid

reactions.mechanism: Cation exchange into clay interlayers concentrates and stabilizes arginine against dilution and degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Although not a net-transformative chemistry step, this node is retained to capture a key literature-supported biochemical consequence: montmorillonite selectively adsorbs arginine far more strongly than neutral amino acids, potentially protecting it after rare formation events. It is included as a process node because preservation is central to arginine plausibility (Catalano et al., 2024).

pathways.name: Core cyanosulfidic arginine-aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Best-supported literature pathway from HCN/acetylene/NH3/H2S under Cu-photoredox, phosphate-buffered, UV-irradiated anaerobic surface conditions to the arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor and then arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with independent cyanamide sourcing
pathways.description: Same constructive surface branch as P1 but explicitly emphasizing photochemical cyanamide generation as a convergent upstream input.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation
pathways.description: Conceptually simplest two-phase route: pre-existing ornithine reacts with cyanamide to form arginine directly.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by clay protection
pathways.description: Arginine formed by direct ornithine guanylation is then concentrated and protected on montmorillonite.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by peptide incorporation
pathways.description: Arginine from ornithine plus cyanamide is subsequently incorporated into a simple peptide product under clay-associated wet-dry cycling.

pathways.name: Speculative prebiotic urea-cycle analog
pathways.description: HCN hydrolysate chemistry supplies cyanate/citrulline pool; ornithine carbamoylation gives citrulline and a hypothetical final conversion gives arginine.

pathways.name: HCN hydrolysate citrulline branch with external cyanamide convergence
pathways.description: A variant speculative branch where HCN hydrolysates generate citrulline-bearing mixtures and independently sourced cyanamide is invoked for final arginine formation.

pathways.name: Trace supercritical pyruvate-hydroxylamine route
pathways.description: Low-confidence literature side branch reproducing the trace arginine report from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine under supercritical CO2/H2O.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal provisioning to speculative guanidino-dicarboxylate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies pyruvate, which converges with guanidine in speculative pool chemistry to a guanidino dicarboxylate analog that thermally decarboxylates to arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface integrated scenario feeding the core cyanosulfidic branch
pathways.description: A fully connected environmental story in which hydrothermal carbon fixation supplies reduced carbon context, surface photochemistry generates cyanamide and the cyanosulfidic arginine precursor, and biochemical hydrolysis yields arginine.
